content: Inside each of our cells, DNA holds the master blueprint for everything the cell needs to do. However, DNA itself never leaves the nucleus, so the cell uses a messenger called mRNA to carry instructions from DNA to the rest of the cell. Think of mRNA as a delivery note. When the cell needs to produce a specific protein, a matching copy of the relevant DNA section is made in the form of mRNA. This mRNA then travels out of the nucleus to the cell’s protein-making machinery. There, the instructions on the mRNA are read and translated into a chain of amino acids, which fold into a functional protein. This process allows the cell to quickly respond to changing needs without exposing the original DNA blueprint. In short, mRNA is the vital courier that carries genetic instructions from storage to production, enabling the cell to function smoothly and adapt as necessary.
